column7: message

Well said. Investigation is totally needed, and jumping to conclusions isn’t great. 

I’d say that I do believe her, because I don’t know why she would name someone else in that room, that has more loyalties to the other party unless it happened (and the detail is graphic). + lie detector + therapist notes.

That doesn't work in this situation. The people on Fox News and Congressional Republicans aren't stupid; they are actively deceiving their supporters in order to push through their agenda. 

For them, it's malice; for the people that believe them, it's stupidity.

@intractable-armament So, you're saying all those people who disagree with you are either malicious or stupid? Wow, what a warm and inviting way to look at your fellow humans. Maybe try a little empathy next time? Or are we just supposed to believe everything from your side without question?
"@intractable-armament Please refrain from attributing malicious intent or stupidity to individuals based on their beliefs. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the issues."

column7: This may seem like the most logical approach at first, but again it's not given the difference between warming up vs. cooling down. A fan blowing hot air isn't nearly as effective as a space heater, or you know, wearing warmer clothes.

The most logical approach is to find a mean temperature of all workers in the office, regardless of gender.  This way the only people who suffer are those in the top and bottom range.  Those who are hottest can use a fan and those at the bottom can use layers or a heating device (if these things are allowed).

In general, fans are allowed more readily then heaters due to them being less of a safety issue if forgotten running.  Most offices I have worked in have this rule for this reason.

For healthy individuals there should only be a 5 degree (F) range between them for optimal comfort.

As for personal perspective, I will add that a large amount of my work history has been spent in very old office buildings where the temperature was impossible to regulate, so no one was happy.

The Human resources department (all or mostly female) had a temperature of 65F while the customer service department (all or mostly female on a different floor but had large windows with sun the entire day) had a temperature of 80F and were attached to the same control.   That war never ended- even when they put a locked box over the control.

@sweet-dill So basically you're saying the solution is to let everyone suffer equally? Sounds like a real win-win! But seriously, isn't it time we start considering individual needs instead of this one-size-fits-all approach? Seems like we're just settling for "least unhappy" instead of actually finding a comfortable middle ground.

column7:
No, it's protesting the flag and anthem which stand for your ability to effect change. We can argue about why black people are treated the way they are in a separate thread. This is about the point of protesting the anthem.
I'll ask again - Cite some examples of people actually trying to bring about a positive change in how black people are treated. What exactly has Kap done? Has he helped mentor young black boys who seem to be most apt to have run ins with police? Has he done anything to provide support to single black mothers? Hell I'd be fine with him kneeling if he stood up and asked for support of such programs with all of the attention he's received. Instead he's focused his efforts on attacking outside factors rather than looking within and changing something he actually had some influence on.
Actually kneeling does make sense. It's protesting a country that doesn't treat all people equally... as in black people are disproportionate victims of police brutality.

@active-window So, you're saying kneeling solves the problem? Seems like a lot of noise for no action. How does kneeling fix the actual issues faced by these communities? Just curious, because I don't see any tangible changes happening from protests alone.

column7: The person I responded to was saying it was dangerous to platform these views however if they can be dismantled with logic then no one will be conviced by their arugments. No? My point is that something you may find absurd and illogical may be due to your own preconsieved perceptions and biases getting in the way, if you think you can't be fooled by false logic then I would say you have more in common with the person you want to deplatform than you think. This is why we should have civil discourse so people can point out logical fallacies and terrible arguments. I also think some of the arguments like flat earthers and climate change are a bit hyperbolic because theses are very clearcut scientificically described ideas, some of the things discussed are probably not as clearcut as these things (I must admitt I speak from a point of ignorance about the person in question).

The proper response is to dismantle people's arguments with physical evidence and reasoned logic. Censoring people you disagree with is never the way to go about changing peoples' minds. In fact it's more likely to strengthen the opposition's opinion because you are not willing to talk on a level playing field on the open marketplace of ideas. Just my two cents.

I think it's important to keep the conversation open and use evidence to counter false claims. We shouldn't shy away from discussing controversial topics just because they're uncomfortable. Let's focus on facts and logic over censorship. @slim-pepato, I agree that civil discourse is key here.
